Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after work or after hanging out with friends, but it rarely happened the way I planned. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep or when panic set in about something being due. I learn best by doing things hands-on, but sitting at a computer watching lectures just made me zone out. I'd often start with good intentions but end up scrolling my phone instead.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd cram like crazy when deadlines were breathing down my neck. I have extracurriculars and other stuff going on, so studying got pushed around a lot. Regular schedules just don't stick for me—I've tried, but life always seems to get in the way. The anxiety about falling behind was constant, but somehow that didn't translate into consistent effort.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting an email reminder that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I missed anything important, scroll around for a bit, then log out without doing much. The gamified tasks they added actually got me logging in a few more times because there was something slightly more interesting to do. But most of the time, it was pure obligation rather than genuine interest.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple weeks where I tried to make it a habit, like checking in every morning with coffee. But that fell apart pretty quickly when things got boring or stressful. The routine never stuck because the platform itself didn't hold my attention—it felt like a chore rather than something I wanted to do. I need more hands-on, interactive stuff to stay engaged.
Q5: The gamified tasks were actually decent—they broke up the monotony and gave me something to do that felt less like traditional studying. I also found the discussion boards somewhat useful when people were actually talking about real problems they were having with the material. Videos were okay when they were short and to the point. Anything that let me interact or do something rather than just passively read was better for my learning style.
Q6: The long reading materials and dense PDFs? Yeah, I basically ignored those. I'd download them thinking I'd read them later, but later never came. The discussion posts that were just people summarizing lectures felt pointless to me, so I skipped those too. I also avoided anything that seemed like busywork—if I couldn't see how it connected to something practical, I just didn't bother. My motivation was already low, so anything that felt tedious got pushed aside.
Q7: It had to feel like it was actually teaching me something I could use, not just checking a box. The gamified stuff worked because there was immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment, even if it was small. If I could see progress or feel like I was building toward something, I'd stick with it longer. But most activities felt disconnected from anything real, which made them feel like a waste of time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, especially when I was already overwhelmed. Required ones at least had the threat of grades attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. But even required stuff, if it felt pointless, I'd do the bare minimum. Optional activities needed to be genuinely interesting for me to bother, and most weren't.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from those on my tablet while lying on my bed or at a coffee shop. Sometimes I'd look stuff up on YouTube because other people explained it better or in a more engaging way. I also talked through concepts with a friend who was in a similar program. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was often more helpful than what I did online.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over downloaded materials or watching external videos to understand a concept, but my LMS activity would show nothing. I'd also sometimes review my handwritten notes from when I actually paid attention in the videos—that review time was invisible. Group study sessions with friends where we'd work through problems together, none of that gets captured either. The LMS only sees what I do on their platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video once, download the slides or take screenshots, and then never watch that video again through the LMS. I'd study from those materials offline, especially when my internet was being slow or I just didn't want to deal with the platform. It was easier to work at my own pace without being connected. So my video watch time probably looked way lower than my actual engagement with that content.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just felt impossible. My anxiety was through the roof, I had other responsibilities piling up, and the course material started getting harder. I'd stare at the screen feeling completely overwhelmed and just... not do anything. The boredom mixed with stress created this paralysis where I knew I should be working but couldn't make myself start. That's when my attendance and engagement really dropped off.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from other stuff or confused about the material, the LMS became the last thing I wanted to deal with. Competing responsibilities—work, extracurriculars, just life—meant the course kept getting deprioritized. When I was confused, I'd avoid the platform because logging in just reminded me how behind I was. The fatigue made everything feel harder than it needed to be.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through content, but my brain was completely elsewhere. I'd watch videos while thinking about other things, or click through readings without absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something productive, even though I wasn't really learning. The high anxiety made it hard to focus even when I tried.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would become next week. Part of it was the anxiety—starting felt overwhelming, so avoiding it was easier in the moment. Some tasks just felt so boring or pointless that I couldn't make myself care enough to start. The procrastination made everything worse, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore stuff until it was almost due, then panic-cram to get something submitted. The LMS would show these spikes of activity right before due dates, then nothing for days. It wasn't healthy, but without that pressure, I had no urgency. The problem was the quality of my work suffered because I was always rushing.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But often the feedback came too late or was too generic to be useful. If I got a bad grade with helpful comments, I'd feel a mix of guilt and determination. But if the feedback was just a number with no explanation, it made me feel like why bother putting in more effort. The gamified tasks gave instant feedback, which was actually more motivating.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with 17 courses worth of content to keep track of. I needed more guidance on what to prioritize, but instead everything felt equally important and equally boring. The flexibility was supposed to be helpful, but for someone like me with low motivation, it just meant I could always put things off. More structured, smaller chunks with clear checkpoints would have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement easier—they gave me small wins and broke up the monotony. But the sheer volume of content and the passive nature of most activities made it harder. Long videos without interaction points lost me quickly. The discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic. If there had been more hands-on, practical activities that matched my kinesthetic learning style, I probably would have engaged more.
Q20: I think that's people like me who are clicking around trying to figure out what's going on, maybe feeling anxious about being behind, but not actually producing work. You can spend a lot of time in the LMS being confused or distracted without it translating to completed assignments. Sometimes high activity is just procrastination disguised as productivity—you're there, but you're not really doing anything meaningful.
Q21: That makes sense to me. Some people are efficient—they know exactly what they need, grab it, and study elsewhere. Or they're naturally smart and don't need to spend hours on the platform to understand the material. I wish I was like that. For me, low activity usually meant low engagement, but I can see how someone more focused could get more done with less visible time online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they can't show if you're actually paying attention or just have the tab open. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do away from the screen. They definitely can't measure understanding or genuine engagement with ideas. It's just surface-level data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, asking questions, thinking about how things connect. For me, it's also about doing something active—not just watching or reading, but applying and practicing. I wasn't really engaged this semester; I was just surviving, barely.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how anxious and overwhelmed I felt most of the time, or how that affected my ability to focus. It wouldn't capture the offline studying I did, or the times I genuinely tried but couldn't make progress. It also wouldn't show that the gamified tasks actually helped my mood and made me feel slightly more connected to the course. My emotional state and the invisible effort—that's all missing from the data.
